sentences.text: Yeah.
We could go--
They came and they took us from the home.
They chased us out from the home like cattles.
Run.
You had to run, not walk, just run.
And then we came to my aunt's factory, where we went there sometime ice skating, because it was a little pond there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
My mother and my sister Serene and Hannah and me.
My grandparents in their own home.
They occupied-- Hungarians occupied my grandparents' home, because they had big homes.
And most of that they left them one room for them, and the whole house was occupied from Hungarian soldiers or families.
I don't know that.
But I know just they had one room.
Each grandparents had one room.
That's all.
You ran.
Everybody, if you could or not, you ran, because they chased you.
You tried your best.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They never let you walk.
About [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] five blocks or six blocks without stopping, running.
And you know that your life is depending on them, because shooting, it was nothing for them.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I came back, I heard that how many people were shot in the city who were arguing that don't-- that they shouldn't take their business away or fighting they shouldn't take looting there.
Right away, they shot them, the Hungarians.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That time, a bullet was just like in this country now, to kill anybody when they want here.
But there, it was just for Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yes.
Yes, a lot of people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And from a big home to be in one room.
And we had even good things, because, like I say, it was my aunt's factory, so-- but the whole family in one room and everything.
You couldn't go out.
You couldn't wash yourself, normally.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just a little water was there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The only thing that we had still to eat-- not big dinners or big lunches or-- to eat to survive, I mean to eat not starving.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Nothing.
Nothing.
Just talking to each other.
What happened with that family or this family?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just waiting your death, when they will come and pick you up and kill you.
That was most of the conversation what we had when they were--
No.
No.
They said that they will take us away from here and they will put us in working camps.
We will go to work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the rumors.
All the Jews.
I don't know how many Jewish people we had in Munkacs that time.
I think it was around 20,000.
I think so.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they were divided in-- in two ghettos in Munkacs.
That's what I know.
So probably 10,000 people were there.
And you just talked about rumors, what goes on.
You didn't know nothing.
Just you heard they shoot that family [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in the city.
So that's all what you talked.
I think that I was the second group, not the first.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I can't remember, sweetheart.
I don't know.
Yes.
My father-- I mean my mother's father and mother, because I went with them to the wagons.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
How I left the ghetto?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They came screaming that we have to leave now.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That-- that's all that I remember.
And they took us.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And they said you could take a suitcase.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My mother had a tiny suitcase just with a nightgown and one dress to change, and so did all of us had that, one change.
And when they chased us from the ghetto already, it looked that it was-- my mother couldn't run, because they chased us-- not walking, just running.
And she stopped already there.
They beat her with a big like a baseball bat in front of me, in front of my sister.
[CRYING] It was terrible to see that, the first time that they hurt my mother.
[CRYING]
Then they chased us in the wagons and they locked us in there with a pail.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my grandfather was there and my grandmother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I saw a lot of people.
My cousin and my aunt from the brick factory, they were there.
My cousins, a lot of people, my relatives.
I can't remember everybody.
I remember my grandfather [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] very well.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't even have enough room to sit there.
Already there it started the trouble in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cattle train.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You couldn't move your legs.
You couldn't move.
The way you sit down, that's the way you were, because like herrings, each-- everybody sat near each other.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I was there and I thought to myself, that's the end.
We all will die.
I didn't even think of that they will kill us there.
I just thought that from the situation, we can't take that, how it is.
You couldn't move.
Your hand got stuck between the other person.
The other person couldn't move either.
It wasn't room for nothing.
It was terrible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think we went three or four days like that.
Food?
No food.
And we had a pail, whoever had to go.
In the smell, you had to breathe and everything.
They didn't take out the pail, nothing.
Food, no.
No water.
No food at all.
Nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
I wasn't even close to them.
My grandfather was close to me.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the smell was terrible.
And my grandfather [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] always liked good smells.
Always we bought him in the springtime those flowers what it smells very good, jasmine, I think, because he loved the good smell.
And the smell was terrible.
And I thought to myself, poor Grandpa.
How can he take that?
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] But we took it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
After that, they let us out.
I heard that a lot of people died in the wagons.
I don't remember if in our car somebody died.
I don't remember that at all.
When the doors were open, they chased us out.
And a good-looking German soldier was there, Mengele, with his riding boots and his riding-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know-- wand or what the heck you call it.
And he just pointed right and left, and that's it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He pointed me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess, the right way.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my sister, too, Serene.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] See, I don't remember.
Now I remember that Hannah was there, too.
[CRYING] Hannah and me went to the right side.
And Serene was also-- she supposed to be on the right side, and she went back, because she didn't want to leave my mother.
And he took her in his hand and pulled her back, and she said no.
She went back twice to my mother to be with my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the rest was history for everybody.
They took away those little suitcases what we had from us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they put us in-- in a room, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] naked.
And they cut our hair.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just woman.
And they gave us clothes that we should look like not human at all.
A thin person got big clothes.
A fat person got [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] little clothes, just to make fun out of us even there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I saw that happen.
And I wanted to go with them and they pushed me back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They pushed me back.
And that's it.
No, no.
Nothing.
Nothing.
No goodbyes.
Nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
After that they gave me the clothes, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
They took us in a room.
And I saw my cousin by whom we been in the ghetto.
She lives.
She's in England.
I saw her.
She was a beautiful girl, her hair shaved.
I says, Hilda, how you look?
I didn't see myself.
I didn't know.
I know what they did with me.
I felt it.
But you-- you didn't know what they've done to you.
But anyway, then we been together.
Then they pushed us in the barracks.
But it was almost the same thing.
On a place 12 people supposed to lay.
We were fighting constantly if somebody moved, because it wasn't place to move or to do something.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] You know, they put the Jews together that they should have right there the misery that they should kill each other.
They shouldn't waste a bullet for us.
Just they wanted we should kill each other.

When one of us started to move, the other 11 started to fight, because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my feet was in somebody's else mouth, because that's the way they lay us down, not that all the heads were on one side, no.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yeah.
No.
Yes.
I was with my sister, Hannah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I knew my nieces-- I mean my cousin's nieces too.
Two of them were in the same bed with us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm trying to think who else was there from Munkacs, [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] because I know we were 12 people there.
But I don't know who was there.
I'm thinking here.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
What you mean, typical?
A day?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They put us out already 5 o'clock in the morning.
And they put us in lines.
I don't know how many people for appell.
And they counted us all the time, all the time.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was their business, to count us.
And in the mud you stood, and rain sometimes, because it was a terrible weather there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just mud I saw in my whole time being in Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never saw a tree or a grass.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
I was working in the kitchen, peeling potatoes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one day, I took-- I saw my cousin's mother there.
And I brought her in in the kitchen.
And I said to her, sit down here.
And they were looking for her the whole day.
And I knew that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they will shoot her and me too, when they find out, because the main thing was that they should have the figures what they had.
They didn't want to lose, God forbid, one person, so-- but they didn't kill us then.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I saved my aunt that time.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I told her to sit down and peel potatoes the same way like I do for that few hours that we had.
You couldn't even touch the-- the potato peel to eat it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I peeled them, because they watched you constantly.
No, no.
Not at all.
[PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS]
I remember when I was peeling the potatoes, I put the potatoes in my dress.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went out and I took it for my cousins.
And you know, the lights were on you constantly, because it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I wasn't afraid.
I had two potatoes.
And that was like I would have a whole bakery with me.
And I wasn't afraid that they will kill me.
No fear at all that time what could happen.
Life is over.
We were praying that life should be over.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
I'm thinking.
I don't remember already.
When was it?
I know it was in Auschwitz.
But when my sister and I supposed to put potatoes in those barrels and put it-- take it together on a-- to put away for the Germans for the winter in the ground.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we stopped, because I said to my sister, I can carry no more.
Let them kill us.
So they let the dogs on us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we stopped.
And the dogs [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were jumping on our faces and scratching us all over.
My legs and my sisters' legs were bleeding, but that's nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And from the dirt, we didn't get no [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] infection there.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Yeah, sure.
They don't let you standing there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
From that.
I prayed to-- for dying all the time, because I heard what happened to my family, that they told us next that-- don't you see the chimney?
They are burned?
They told us what happened to them.
Don't look for them, because they are not alive anymore, whoever was-- you are alive, and that's it.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We couldn't believe it, but those girls who came from Poland who were there already two years, they used to tell us that you were still sitting by a table and eating normal food.
We are here already three or four years.
We build all the barracks.
You have it very good now.
They said that we had it good now.
I says, you're talking like it was our fault that we remain until now.
She says, no.
But you don't know what we went through here [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to build all the barracks.
They had to do that.
So anyway, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
You know, I believe even now-- always I believed in fate.
When you are born, it's written what will happen to your life.
I always believed that.
And you can do nothing against it.
It was meant for me to survive, because I wasn't even in the city there in Budapest.
They didn't take people on the street that time to put them in ghettos and all those things.
So anyway-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I got the stroke, I believed in that, too.
God had it for me that I should go through some more miseries here, too.
I went plenty in this country.
You could say it's [NON-ENGLISH].
